speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewee
text: He's very interested in the Holocaust. He asks me a lot of questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you--what do you know about how the Holocaust? What do your grandparents tell you about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: That there was a war. That the Germans wanted some land, and they wanted all the Jews to get out. So they-- they made a war.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very lucky that your grandparents survived, weren't you? Jeff, as you were growing up, did you feel different than other kids because your parents were survivors? Did they somehow send you a different kind of message?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the neighborhood that we grew up in was really-- the people that we associated with probably mostly Holocaust survivors. So certainly during my early childhood, I don't-- I don't think that I sensed feeling different. I probably didn't feel different until I got older, and moved away, and realized that many other people really did have living grandparents. I think I felt a difference from my parents, though, early on in that-- there was a feeling of-- that my parents sacrificed a lot-- a lot for us. They put a lot into their children, because that was really all they had left. Family was very important to-- very important to them. And I still feel that to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sense a bitterness in them because of what they'd lost?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, I think there are certainly-- you know, certainly bad feelings towards the people that did it. But I don't feel that bitterness spilled over into the way that they-- they conducted their lives or the way they raised their families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Robert, will you tell us briefly about how Sundays are spent in this family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sundays, we all pretty much do things independently in the mornings, but afternoons, it's usually pizza dinner with Bubby and Zayde and all the cousins. It's a very special time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're a very special family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Victor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is my paternal grandfather. His name is Jacob Ungar. He lived in the city of Bratislava. The photo must've been taken in the 1930s. That's how I remembered him. This is my paternal grandmother. I never called her by other name than grandmother. That's why I don't know her first name. The picture was taken in the city of Bratislava-- that's the capitol of Slovakia. Must've been taken in the 1930s. This is the picture of my grandfather, Jacob Ungar, to the right of me. And the left of me is his brother-- older brother by the name of Labe Ungar. It was taken in the city of Humenne. That's in eastern Slovakia. They were studying in the yeshiva at that time. The photo must've been taken in the 1870s. This is Esther Halpert, my maternal grandmother. She lived in Medzilaborce, and the picture must have been taken in the early 1930s. This picture is of my father as a baby, Eugene Ungar. It was taken in the city of Kormano. That's southwestern Slovakia. Must have been taken in 1998.

speaker: interviewee
text: 18-- I'm sorry, 1898. This is my father. His name was Eugene Ungar. This picture was taken in Budapest in 1916, or '17. He served in the Hungarian-Austrian army. He was-- at that time, he was at the defense ministry, working as a clerk. He attained the rank of a Master Sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us again how you managed to keep these-- to find these photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: These photographs that we displayed right now-- I found them right after the war, in our apartment. My father arranged with the county president to keep the apartment from being given away to someone else. And he kept his promise. This picture is of my mother. Her name was Theresa Ungar. This must have been taken in the city of Kosice in the 19-- early 1920s. This picture was-- pictures her as a bride. And since my paternal grandparents were very religious, they smeared some paint over her decote attire. This is the picture of my father, Eugene Ungar. It was taken it Kosice in 19-- early 1940s. This is my mother. Her name was Theresa Ungar. Was taken in Kosice in the early '30s. 1930S. This picture is of myself to the left, Victor. Middle one is my brother, Armand. The third one is my sister, Claire. And the two ladies are the two nannies that took care of us. It was taken in Kosice, Czechoslovakia in the late-- probably 1929. This picture show-- pictures me from left to right. I was 12 years old. Next to me is my father, Eugene Ungar. Next to my father is my mother, Theresa Ungar. Next to them, there are two cousins of my cousin. Their names were Klein-- the fellow's name and the young lady's name was Klein. On the extreme right is the picture of my cousin who lived in Brooklyn, Elias Klein. This picture was taken in 1937, in the city of Krynica, Poland. It's a resort place. This picture is of our family. From right to left, on top is my son, Jeffrey Ungar. To his right is my picture. On the bottom row, from left is my son, Robert Ungar. In the middle is my wife, Gloria. To the right of my wife, Gloria, is Michael Ungar, my youngest son. It was taken in 1975 in Los Angeles. This picture of-- is the children-- are the grandchildren of my son. They were not seen before. They live in Teaneck, New Jersey. From the left is Elana Ungar. She is seven years old. To her right is Shoshana Ungar. She's nine years old. In the middle is the youngest sister, Elisheva, holding that little teddy bear. It was taken in 1992. These are my grandchildren, the children of Robert Ungar. Top row is my grandson, Dovy. To the right is Deena. In the middle is Ahai. And the youngest one-- his name is Shlomo. He was here before. He was taken-- when my son was holding him on his lap. It was taken in 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Los Angeles.
